title: Drum carder colour blending
subtitle: Loading and blending multiple colours on a drum carder to produce a heathered or gradient batt
excerpt: A drum carder opens and blends fibres from different colours into a single batt ready for spinning or felting. Loading the colours in different orders and amounts produces different results: stripes, heathered blends, and gradients. This guide covers the basic loading technique and three colour approaches.
sourceNotes: Drum carder colour blending is standard fibre preparation practice. Method is observation-led, consistent with guild workshop instruction.

content.text: to the main drum as the handle is cranked. Loading different colours at different points in the cranking sequence determines how they blend. All three methods below use the same tool; only the loading sequence changes.

attrs.body: Carding throws fibre dust. Work in a ventilated room. Damp-mop after; never dry-sweep.
attrs.title: Wool dust

items.name: Washed fleece or commercial roving in 2-3 colours
Doffer (a thin stick or the carder's own doffer)

content.text: Hold a small amount of fibre against the licker-in drum and crank slowly. The teeth catch the fibre and transfer it to the main drum. Load evenly from left to right to keep the batt uniform in thickness. Load in thin layers; overloading produces a dense, poorly opened batt. When the main drum is full (covered with a thin even layer), stop.

Load colour A, then colour B, then colour A again, alternating thin layers. Pass the batt off the carder and load it again in the same alternating pattern. After two passes, the colours are well blended and will spin to a heathered mix.

Load colour A in the first quarter of the main drum (cranking slowly while holding colour A to the licker-in until that section fills). Then load colour B for the middle half. Load colour A again for the final quarter. When the batt is removed, it shows a gradient: A at one end, B in the middle, A at the other end. Do not re-card: the gradient is produced by spatial separation on the drum, not blending.

Load colour A across the full left half of the drum and colour B across the full right half. The finished batt will show two side-by-side colour stripes. Used for weaving weft, the stripes produce a self-striping yarn; used for felting, the batt produces a two-colour panel.

items.fix: Load in very thin, even layers from left to right, cranking slowly as you feed. Watch the drum surface and stop adding when it reaches an even coverage.
items.cause: Uneven loading: too much fibre in some areas, too little in others
items.problem: The batt has thick and thin patches

items.fix: Remove the batt and load it again, pulling it into small pieces before re-loading to randomise the colour placement.
items.cause: Only one pass through the carder
items.problem: Colour blending is patchy rather than even in the heathered blend

items.fix: Tear roving into short sections (5-8 cm) before loading. Hold fibre parallel to the licker-in surface, not perpendicular.
items.cause: Fibre fed in too long a piece or at too steep an angle
items.problem: Fibres wind around the licker-in drum and jam
